content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 40 (GEN_040)

## 1. Chateu — "Offended" vs. "Sinned Against" (v. 1)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "offended" | Captures the courtly register of the offense |
| LOGOS | "sinned against" | Preserves the theological weight of chata' — connecting to 39:9 |
| PNEUMA | "gave offense to" | Dynamic equivalent of EMET |
| **SOPHIA** | **"offended" — EMET upheld** | The root chata' carries theological weight in 39:9 but here the context is courtly — an offense against a king's dignity, not a sin against God. "Offended" captures the register without over-theologizing. The verbal echo with 39:9 is preserved through the shared root, but the English rendering should reflect the different context. |

## 2. Zo'afim — "Troubled" vs. "Downcast" (vv. 6–7)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "troubled" | za'af = vexed, troubled, agitated |
| LOGOS | "downcast" | Captures the visible emotional state |
| PNEUMA | "downcast" | Same dynamic rendering |
| **SOPHIA** | **"troubled" — EMET upheld** | zo'afim describes emotional disturbance — agitation, vexation — not merely sadness. "Downcast" implies only low spirits. "Troubled" captures the broader range of the Hebrew. |

## 3. Sallei Chori — "Baskets of White Bread" vs. "Wicker Baskets" (v. 16)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "baskets of white bread" | chori from chur = "white" — describes content |
| LOGOS | "wicker baskets" | chori from root for "perforated/woven" — describes material |
| PNEUMA | "baskets of white bread" | Same as EMET |
| **SOPHIA** | **"baskets of white bread" — 2-1** | Both etymologies are possible. LXX reads χονδριτῶν ("coarse bread"), supporting the bread-content reading. The context (a baker's dream about bread) also favors the content reading. |

---

*Genesis 40: 3 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with EMET on all 3 items (vv. 1, 6, 16). This was the most harmonious chapter yet in the Joseph cycle — the three translators agreed on all major theological and structural decisions (pitronim, bor, chesed, gunnov gunnabti, yissa roshekha). The few disagreements are matters of register and etymology rather than theology or narrative interpretation.*
